cards.q: What are the main nuclear processes discussed in this chapter?
cards.a:
• $\alpha$ decay
• $\beta^-$ decay
• $\beta^+$ decay
• Electron capture (EC)
• $\gamma$ emission
• Internal conversion
• Auger electron emission
• Neutron production (spontaneous fission and nuclear reactions)

cards.q: Classify the main radioactive emissions as charged particles, neutral particles, or photons.
cards.a:
Charged particles:
• $\alpha$
• $\beta^-$
• $\beta^+$
• Internal conversion electrons
• Auger electrons

Neutral particles:
• Neutrons
• Neutrinos

Photons:
• $\gamma$ rays
• Characteristic X-rays
• 511 keV annihilation photons

cards.q: Compare the principal radioactive decay modes.
cards.a:
| Process | Physical change |
|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | Emits a $^4$He nucleus |
| $\beta^-$ | $n\rightarrow p$ |
| $\beta^+$ | $p\rightarrow n$ |
| Electron capture | $p+e^-\rightarrow n$ |
| $\gamma$ | Nuclear de-excitation |

cards.q: Which emissions are monoenergetic, and which have continuous energy spectra?
cards.a:
Monoenergetic:
• $\alpha$
• $\gamma$
• Internal conversion electrons
• Auger electrons
• Characteristic X-rays
• Annihilation photons

Continuous:
• $\beta^-$
• $\beta^+$
• Neutron spectra from most laboratory sources

cards.q: What are the characteristic experimental signatures of the different radioactive processes?
cards.a:
• Electron capture → characteristic X-rays or Auger electrons
• Positron emission → two 511 keV annihilation photons
• $\gamma$ emission → discrete photon lines
• Internal conversion → monoenergetic electrons
• Auger emission → low-energy monoenergetic electrons

cards.q: Order the main radiations by increasing penetrating power.
What are the main laboratory neutron sources, and what are their advantages?
cards.a:
• Spontaneous fission (e.g. $^{252}$Cf): simple, broad neutron spectrum.
• $(\alpha,n)$ sources (e.g. Am-Be): high neutron yield, broad spectrum.
• $(\gamma,n)$ sources: lower neutron yield but nearly monoenergetic neutrons.
